author: William James
title: The Will to Believe and Other Essays in Popular Philosophy
relevance: Includes The Dilemma of Determinism, The Will to Believe, and Great Men and Their Environment.

author: T. H. Huxley
title: On the Hypothesis That Animals Are Automata, and Its History
relevance: Original formulation of conscious automatism and its physiological evidence.

author: Charles Darwin
title: The Descent of Man, and Selection in Relation to Sex
relevance: Continuity of human and animal mental faculties and the evolution of moral and intellectual powers.

author: John Stuart Mill
title: Considerations on Representative Government
relevance: Fitness for self-government, participation, political education, and the franchise.

author: John Stuart Mill
title: The Subjection of Women
relevance: Legal subordination, government of one's own person, and women's political capacity.

author: Karl Marx
title: Capital, Volume I, Chapter 15: Machinery and Modern Industry
relevance: Machinery, factory discipline, displacement, and the worker's relation to the production process.

author: William Morris
title: Useful Work versus Useless Toil
relevance: Meaningful work, toil, machinery, and the social conditions of liberation from labor.

author: Frederick Winslow Taylor
title: The Principles of Scientific Management
relevance: Task decomposition, managerial control over method and pace, and worker discretion.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The World Set Free
relevance: Civilization-scale technological danger and atomic war.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The Time Machine
relevance: Narrative vision of cosmic decline and the dying Earth.

author: H. G. Wells
title: The War in the Air
relevance: Technological power turning cities and civilization into targets.

author: Gustave Le Bon
title: The Crowd: A Study of the Popular Mind
relevance: Individual judgment and agency in a psychological crowd.

author: Sigmund Freud
title: Group Psychology and the Analysis of the Ego
relevance: Identification, suggestion, and altered individual agency in groups.

author: Walter Lippmann
title: Public Opinion
relevance: Pseudo-environments, stereotypes, news, and the formation of public opinion.

author: Edward Bernays
title: Propaganda
relevance: Deliberate organization of public habits and opinions.

author: Mary Shelley
title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus (1818 edition)
relevance: Maker responsibility and the creature's demand for companionship; a labeled pre-1860 anchor.

author: E. M. Forster
title: The Eternal Moment and Other Stories
relevance: Includes The Machine Stops: infrastructural dependence, isolation, machine worship, and collapse.

author: Yevgeny Zamyatin
title: We
relevance: Numbered citizens, scheduled life, regulated intimacy, and dangerous imagination.

author: Karel Capek
title: R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)
relevance: Artificial workers, exploitation, personhood, and revolt.

author: Samuel Butler
title: Erewhon
relevance: Machine evolution, dependence, succession, and destruction of machines.

author: William James
title: The Principles of Psychology, Volume 1
relevance: Habit, automaton theory, brain function, and association.

author: William James
title: The Principles of Psychology, Volume 2
relevance: Emotion, competing desires, effort of attention, and will.

author: Henry George
title: Progress and Poverty
relevance: Poverty amid technological progress and the social production and appropriation of wealth.

author: Bertrand Russell
title: A Free Man's Worship, in Modern Essays
relevance: Human value and action in an indifferent, ultimately lifeless universe.

author: Eduardo Ladislao Holmberg
title: Horacio Kalibang o los automatas
relevance: Artificial persons and automata used to stage a materialist-spiritualist dispute in late nineteenth-century Argentina.

author: Didier de Chousy
title: Ignis
relevance: Automated industry, manufactured workers, capitalist control, labor conflict, and technological catastrophe.

author: Miguel de Unamuno
title: Mecanopolis
relevance: A city populated and governed only by machines, presented as both alluring and intolerably inhuman.
provenanceRelationship: Explicit literary response to Samuel Butler's Erewhon, which the opening paragraph names; treat as reception and transformation, not independent convergence.

author: Paolo Mantegazza
title: L'anno 3000
relevance: Artificial vital power, planetary infrastructure, medical governance, thought-reading, and the gap between technical and moral progress.

author: Valery Bryusov
title: The Republic of the Southern Cross
relevance: A highly administered industrial republic whose apparent order collapses through collective behavioral breakdown.

author: Frigyes Karinthy
title: Utazas Faremidoba
relevance: A society of intelligent machine beings that regards organic humanity and war as diseased forms of life.

author: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain
title: Sultana's Dream
relevance: A feminist technological utopia in which solar power, scientific education, and altered gender relations are inseparable.

author: Wakizo Hosoi
title: Sagyo Kikai (Work Machine)
relevance: A proletarian poem in which the machine does not liberate the operator: the worker becomes part of its mechanism and is physically consumed by it.

author: Wu Jianren
title: Xin Shitou Ji (New Story of the Stone)
relevance: A late-Qing technological future used to ask how scientific capacity, political order, and national self-determination fit together.

author: Jerzy Zulawski
title: Stara Ziemia (The Old Earth)
relevance: Technological modernity, social control, automation, and estrangement in the final volume of the Lunar Trilogy.

author: Victor Anestin
title: In anul 4000 sau o calatorie la Venus
relevance: A Romanian technological future and voyage to Venus, useful for testing non-Anglophone ideas of progress and planetary civilization.

author: Rodolfo Teofilo
title: O Reino de Kiato
relevance: A Brazilian planned utopia connecting public health, eugenics, education, governance, and personal freedom.

author: Ernst Kapp
title: Grundlinien einer Philosophie der Technik
relevance: Technology as organ projection: machines understood through the human body rather than as an autonomous external species.

author: Riichi Yokomitsu
title: Kikai (Machine)
relevance: Factory work and suspicion rendered through an unseen social machine that measures people and drives their actions; included as the endpoint of the 1860–1930 window.

author: Horacio Quiroga (published as S. Fragoso Lima)
title: El hombre artificial
relevance: A Buenos Aires magazine serial about manufacturing a human capable of sensation, thought, and suffering; obscure within a famous author's oeuvre.

author: Karel Capek
title: Injo Nodongja (Artificial Laborer)
relevance: A Korean translation of R.U.R., serialized in Kaebyok from February through May 1925; valuable for tracing colonial reception, but not an independent historical source for the idea.
provenanceRelationship: Translation of R.U.R.; exclude from counts of independent convergence.
